title: Chapter 16: The Night
content: The apartment is wrong.

Not wrong like something's off — wrong like everything I assumed was a lie. Julian B's penthouse occupies the top floor of a Haussmann building on the Rue de Rivoli, and I'd braced myself for exactly the kind of space that money usually builds: all glass and ego, everything curated to perform wealth at you until you submission. Marble floors. Statement furniture. Art chosen by committee and installed by someone who didn't care.
This is not that apartment.

I'm early — twenty minutes early, because apparently even in Timeline B I can't shake the anxiety of being late to something that matters. He texted me the code to the private entrance *en confiance, just us*, and the words sat in my chest like something warm and dangerous all day. I punched in the numbers, rode the elevator up through six floors of gilt and silence, and stepped into a conspiracy.

Books. Books on every surface — stacked on the coffee table in precarious towers, wedged between couch cushions, open and face-down on the kitchen counter like he'd been reading three at once and refused to choose. The shelves aren't organized by color or size or any design principle I can identify; they're organized by whatever he felt like reaching for next. A record player sits in the corner, the kind with a real needle, and beside it a wooden crate of vinyl so worn the spines are sun-bleached. Plants line the windowsills — actual living plants, jade and pothos and something tall and leafy I can't name, their soil dark and damp like someone tends them with regular hands.

The kitchen is small. Granite countertops, copper pans hanging from a rack, a dish towel slung over the oven handle. There's a half-drunk cup of coffee by the stove and a sticky note on the fridge that says *call Maman* in handwriting so messy it might be a doctor's prescription.

And over the fireplace — one painting. Just one. A woman's back, rendered in gold leaf and grief. Her spine is a ridge of light against darkness, her head turned just enough that you can't see her face, only the curve of her neck, the fall of her hair. The gold catches the lamplight and holds it. She's the loneliest thing in a room full of signs of life, and I can't stop looking at her.
I hear the door before I can untangle why the painting makes my throat tight.

"Avery." He says my name like it's a word he's been practicing. Paper bags rustle in his arms — two of them, the heavy brown kind from a boulangerie, not the sleek logo bags of someone who has assistants for this. His hair is damp at the temples, like he rushed here from somewhere else, and he's wearing a linen shirt with the sleeves rolled to his elbows and — are those flour smudges on the cuff?
"You're early," he says, and smiles. Not the sphinx smile. Something smaller. Realer.
"You're late," I say.
"I had to stop for shallots." He holds up one of the bags like evidence. "The ones at the marché this morning were criminal. I had to go to Ahmed's place on the Rue Montorgueil."
"You have a shallot guy."

"Everyone should have a shallot guy." He sets the bags on the counter and starts unpacking — butter, a baguette still warm enough to fog the paper, a wedge of Comté, a bottle of Sancerre, a handful of thyme that smells like a garden in Provence, and yes, shallots, small and purple and perfect. He moves around his own kitchen like someone who actually uses it, opening drawers and reaching for knives without looking, and I lean against the counter with the wine he's already poured me and watch.

It's absurd. This man could buy half of Paris — I've seen the portfolio, the holdings, the silent empire of Julian B's investments — and here he is, chopping shallots in a linen shirt, his brow slightly furrowed in concentration, his fingers curled just so to protect the tips as the knife moves in quick, precise arcs. The shallots hit a pan of hot butter and sizzle, and the sound fills the small kitchen, and the smell — sharp and sweet and golden — wraps around me like something I didn't know I was hungry for.
"Something funny?" he asks without looking up.

"I'm just — " I take a sip of wine. The Sancerre is cold and grape-bright, mineral at the finish, exactly the kind of thing that should be served by a sommelier in a restaurant with linen napkins and a tasting menu. Instead I'm drinking it in a kitchen where a billionaire is making me dinner with his own hands. "Three Julians," I say. "Three lives. And this one is the one who cooks."

He glances at me, and something flickers across his face — not confusion, but a shade of it, like I've said something that almost fits into a drawer he can't quite open. Then it's gone, and he's adding thyme to the pan, and the fragrance blooms upward, green and warm.
"I find it honest," he says. "Cooking. You start with raw things. You transform them. You eat. It has a beginning, a middle, and an end. Most of life isn't that clean."
I think about timelines. About loops. About how nothing in my life has had a clean ending yet.
"Most of life isn't," I agree.
Dinner is simple and perfect.

He makes a risotto — not the restaurant kind that arrives under a silver cloche with a sprig of something architectural on top. This is risotto the way someone makes it when they've made it a hundred times: patient, stirred slowly, the rice yielding to the broth in its own time. The shallots are in it. The Comté melts through it in pale ribbons. The baguette is for tearing with our hands, and we do, and the crust shatters against my teeth in a way that feels unbearably intimate for something that cost two euros.

We eat at a small table by the window, not the formal dining room I glimpsed through a doorway — that room is dark, unused, haunted by the ghost of dinners he didn't want to host. The record player is on, and Nina Simone's voice fills the apartment with warm crackle and genius, the vinyl's imperfections pressing small static kisses between the notes.
We talk about everything except what matters.

Art — he has opinions, real ones, not the performative kind where you name-drop Kusama and Basquiat and call it taste. He loves Remedios Varo. He thinks the Pompidou is overrated but goes anyway because the building itself makes him feel like the future might still happen. He collects first editions of Borges, and when he says it, I grip my wine glass a little harder and try very hard not to think about labyrinths, about forks in roads, about men who dream other men into existence.
"The Aleph," he says. "The idea that a single point contains every other point in the universe. That you could look into one place and see everything. Isn't that what art is supposed to do?"
My mouth is dry. "Or what love is supposed to do."
He looks at me. Holds it. Then: "Yes. That too."

He tells me about a gallery he wants to open. Small. Independent. Tucked into the Marais somewhere, a space that doesn't care about provenance or auction records or whether a collector's name will look good on a press release. Artists no one knows yet. Painters who work in their kitchens. Sculptors who can't afford studios, so they build things on their balconies and hope the neighbors don't complain.
"I want to find the people who are making something true," he says, "and give them a room where truth is enough."

The candle between us shivers. His eyes are dark and certain and I realize, with the kind of clarity that breaks things open: Julian A wants to conquer the world. Julian C wants to survive it. Julian B wants to make it beautiful.
Same soul. Different prayers.
After dinner we move to the window.

The city spreads below us like a circuit board made of light — the Seine a dark artery, the bridges stitching the banks together in bright sutures, the Tour Eiffel pulsing its slow beacon from the south. I stand with the glass cool against my palm and watch Paris be impossibly, brutally beautiful, and I think about how in every timeline this city is beautiful but it hits different here, tonight, with the taste of his cooking still on my tongue and Nina Simone somewhere behind me singing about the sea.

He stands behind me. Not touching. But close enough that I can feel the warmth of him — the heat that comes off a body, that proof-of-life infrared signature that says *here, here, I'm here*. He's close enough that if I leaned back, my shoulder blades would meet his chest. I don't lean back. But I don't move away.
"I see you," he says.

The words land like stones in still water. The same words from the gallery, that first night, when he looked at me like I was a painting he wanted to read and I felt the dangerous pull of being perceived. But different now. Weighted. The gallery was intel; this is testimony.

"Not the version you perform," he continues. His voice is low and even and he's still not touching me, and somehow the absence of contact is its own kind of touch — the air between us charged, humming, a wire pulled taut. "The other one. The one who's afraid."
I go very still.
"I don't know what you're running from, Avery." He says it gently, like he's naming something fragile. "But this — right now — you're not running."

Below us, Paris breathes. A siren threads through the distant streets. The window glass holds the ghost of my reflection — transparent, overlaid on all those lights — and I think about how that's what I am now. A ghost overlaid on other lives. A transparency that used to be a person.
I turn to face him.

The space between us is nothing. The space between us is everything. He doesn't move. He's waiting — and the waiting is the thing, the thing that undoes me, because Julian Deveraux has been choosing everything since the moment I met him in any version of this story, but right now he's choosing not to choose for me. He's giving me the agency. The yes or no. The hand that reaches or doesn't.
For someone who could own the room with a glance, he's remarkably unwilling to own this.
I raise my hand.

My fingers find his jaw. The skin is warm and smooth, slightly rough along the grain of evening stubble. I trace the line of it — the angle, the bone beneath — and my thumb passes over the place where Julian C has a scar. A clean slice of healed tissue, a story written in skin.
Here, there's nothing. Smooth. Intact. No scar.
But I know, in my bones, in the marrow-deep certainty that lives below language, that it's the same face. Same man. Same gravity pulling me into the same orbit.
"I'm not running," I whisper.

The words come out so quiet I'm not sure I said them until I feel the breath of them leave my lips. The most honest thing I've said to any Julian in any timeline. Maybe the most honest thing I've said to anyone, ever.
Something shifts in his eyes. Not triumph — something rawer. Something that looks like hope held carefully, the way you hold a bird that's flown into your window and might not survive the night.
He kisses me.

It's slow. Deliberate. Like every other thing Julian Deveraux does — patient, thorough, and devastating. His hand comes up to cradle the back of my head, his fingers threading into my hair, and his mouth is warm and certain and tasting of Sancerre, and I kiss him back and think — I'm kissing one of three men who are all the same man and I belong to none of them and all of them and this is either the most beautiful thing that's ever happened to me or the beginning of something I won't survive.

The record has stopped. I didn't notice when. The apartment is silent except for our breathing, the small sounds of fabric shifting, the brush of his linen shirt against my fingertips where my hand is still pressed to his jaw. He tastes like wine and patience and like someone who has been waiting for this, waiting for me, and the weight of that waiting should be too much but it isn't — it's just enough, just right, just the pressure of him against my mouth saying the things his restraint won't let him say any other way.

When we pull apart, his forehead rests against mine. His breath is warm on my face. I can feel his heartbeat through his shirt, or maybe that's mine, or maybe it's both of ours, indistinguishable now.
"Reste," he says.
Stay.
One word in French that means everything.
I stay.

The bedroom is as cluttered as the rest of the apartment — books on the nightstand, a sweater draped over a chair, the sheets not perfectly tucked because someone actually sleeps here. The record player's needle is still stuck in the run-out groove, clicking softly in the other room, a tiny mechanical heartbeat. He doesn't get up to fix it, and neither do I.

We don't talk about what this means. We don't talk about the morning. We lie in the warm wreckage of his bed and his arm is around me and the city light comes through the curtains in pale stripes across his chest, and I press my nose to the linen of his skin and smell wine and woodsmoke and something green — thyme, maybe, the ghost of dinner still clinging to him — and I listen to his breathing slow into sleep.
I stay awake longer than I should.

I stay awake because I know what the morning will bring. I know that when the sun comes up and Paris turns from gold to gray, my other lives will start calling — the phone buzzing with Julian A's texts, the ache in my chest that belongs to Julian C's timeline, the Fork app and its cold arithmetic. I know I'll have to leave this bed and this man and this version of the story that let me, for one night, be someone who isn't running.
I know all of that. And I stay anyway.
The Fork app glows on the nightstand.
INTEGRITY: 54%.

Below sixty percent for the first time. The number sits there like a diagnosis, like a verdict, like the first crack in a dam. I should feel it — the weight of it, the warning. I should get up. I should open the app and check the logs and try to understand what's collapsing and where.
Instead I close my eyes.
His arm tightens around me, just slightly, in sleep — like even unconscious, he knows I'm here. Like even unconscious, he's asking me to stay.
The night holds us both, and the app glows, and somewhere in the dark architecture of the multiverse the walls are coming down.
I've never been happier.
I've never been more afraid.
